dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for a male patient with breathing difficulties.
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
I'm having trouble breathing. It started this morning and has gotten worse.
He's alert and oriented, AAO X4. Glasgow Coma Scale is 15.
Patient is in moderate to major distress. Lung sounds show rales and rhonchi.
Airway is patent and maintainable. Patient's preferred posture is upright.
Skin is warm, dry, and normal in appearance.
Pulse is 118, respiratory rate is 32, and blood pressure is 217/134.
Oxygen saturation is 88% on room air. Blood glucose is 255 mg/dL.
The patient reported recently having a heart attack and three strokes a few weeks ago.
There is no reported pain. Breathing difficulty has been progressively worsening since this morning.
Pupils are equal, round, and reactive. Symmetric facial features.
Pulses, motor, and sensation are good in all extremities.
No edema, deformity, or trauma noted.
An IV was successfully started in the left antecubital vein using an 18-gauge catheter.
A 4-lead ECG was initiated. Rhythm is sinus tachycardia.
A 12-lead ECG was performed. Findings include left atrial enlargement and left bundle branch block.
We administered 40 mg of furosemide IVP.
We gave 0.4 mg of nitroglycerin sublingually. Improved breathing reported.
Oxygen saturation improved to 96% with non-rebreather at 12 liters.
Blood pressure was 210/91 en route.
Rales heard in all lung fields.
No rashes, bruises, or scars. Skin is normal with good turgor.
No obstruction, foreign bodies, or drainage noted in ears, nose, or mouth.
Medical history includes primary hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and type two diabetes insulin-dependent.
Mental status is normal with intact short- and long-term memory.
No pain, tenderness, rigidity, or masses noted in the abdomen.
Patient's choice to transport to [COMPANY_NAME].
Patient was secured on the stretcher with all straps.
We transmitted the report to ED staff in an EDTA bed.
Respiratory rate decreased to 28 following intervention.
Oxygen saturation stabilized at 96% during reassessment.
Blood pressure was 210/91.
Medication list includes Alprazolam, Lantus, Lipitor, Lisinopril, Melatonin, and Trazodone.
Allergies are to Penicillin and Prednisone.
EKG findings and field impression of acute pulmonary edema were relayed to nursing.
Patient entered the hospital and was transferred to the ED bed using full lift.
Rechecked pulse, respirations, and BP. Results remained the same.
No repeat medications or interventions were required during transport.
Patient was safely and securely transferred to the hospital bed.
Report includes patient self-discharge instructions and medication history provided to nurse.
Protocol 2-3 (Pulmonary Edema/CHF) was followed throughout this call.
Patient left the scene and was placed into the [COMPANY_NAME] hospital care.
Per IVC, PMS remained intact in all four extremities.
Initial sats of 88 on RA, increased to 97 with NRB.
